The Empire trilogy, co-authored with Negri, argues that the classical imperialism of nation-states has been superseded by a decentered, networked form of global sovereignty that Hardt and Negri call Empire. Against this stands the multitude, a new revolutionary subject composed of diverse singularities whose immaterial and affective labor creates the common wealth that Empire seeks to expropriate. Their work has been both celebrated for its ambitious rethinking of power and resistance in the era of globalization and criticized for underestimating the continued importance of nation-states and traditional class formations.

name: Michael Heinrich
key_works: An Introduction to the Three Volumes of Karl Marx's Capital
body: Michael Heinrich is a leading figure in the German "neue Marx-Lekture" (new Marx reading), which reinterprets Marx's value theory as fundamentally a monetary theory in which value has no pre-monetary existence independent of the exchange process. Drawing extensively on the Marx-Engels-Gesamtausgabe (MEGA) critical edition, he has challenged traditional Marxist readings on the tendency of the rate of profit to fall, the role of abstract labor, and the status of Marx's unfinished manuscripts. His introductory text on the three volumes of Capital has become a standard reference in value-form theoretical approaches.

name: Michael Löwy
key_works: The Theory of Revolution in the Young Marx
body:
Michael Lowy is a Brazilian-French sociologist and philosopher who has made wide-ranging contributions to Marxist theory, from studies of the young Marx's revolutionary thought to analyses of romanticism, religion, and ecosocialism. A research director at the French National Centre for Scientific Research, he has worked to recover neglected dimensions of the Marxist tradition, particularly the romantic critique of capitalism and the intersection of Marxism with liberation theology.

In The Theory of Revolution in the Young Marx, Lowy traced the formation of Marx's revolutionary perspective, while Fire Alarm offered an influential reading of Walter Benjamin's theses on history as a critique of progressive, linear conceptions of historical development. Lowy has been a leading proponent of ecosocialism, arguing that ecological crisis demands a fundamental rethinking of Marxist categories of production and progress. His work bridges Latin American, European, and critical theory traditions, emphasizing the elective affinities between revolutionary romanticism and Marxist critique.

name: Nicos Poulantzas
body: Nicos Poulantzas was a Greek-French political sociologist who developed one of the most influential Marxist theories of the capitalist state. Drawing on Althusser's structuralism, he argued that the state possesses relative autonomy from the dominant class, functioning to organize the long-term interests of the power bloc while disorganizing the working class. His later work moved toward a relational theory of the state as a condensation of class forces, influencing subsequent debates on state theory, authoritarianism, and democratic socialism.

name: Paul Baran
body:
Paul Baran was a Russian-born American Marxist economist who taught at Stanford University and was a central figure in the Monthly Review school of Marxist thought. His work focused on the political economy of development and the structural mechanisms by which advanced capitalist countries maintained the underdevelopment of the Third World.

In The Political Economy of Growth (1957), Baran introduced the concept of "potential economic surplus" to explain how imperialism, feudal remnants, and monopoly power prevent underdeveloped countries from utilizing their productive capacities. His collaboration with Paul Sweezy produced Monopoly Capital (1966), which argued that mature capitalism is characterized by giant corporations that generate a surplus far exceeding profitable investment opportunities, leading to stagnation, waste, and militarism. Baran's work provided the theoretical foundations for dependency theory and for the Monthly Review tradition's analysis of imperialism, monopoly, and economic stagnation.

name: Paul Mattick
body:
Paul Mattick was a German-born, American-based Marxist theorist who combined council communist politics with a rigorous defense of Marx's crisis theory grounded in the tendency of the rate of profit to fall. Active in the German workers' movement as a teenager during the revolutionary upheavals of 1918-21, Mattick emigrated to the United States in 1926, where he became involved in the IWW and the council communist movement, editing the journal Living Marxism (later New Essays) through the 1930s and 1940s.

His major work, Marx and Keynes (1969), argues that Keynesian demand management and state intervention cannot overcome the fundamental contradictions of capital accumulation but merely postpone and intensify crisis. Mattick contends that the postwar boom, far from refuting Marx's crisis theory, was sustained by war spending, imperial expansion, and credit creation that temporarily offset the underlying decline in profitability — measures that would eventually exhaust themselves and produce a return of systemic crisis. Economic Crisis and Crisis Theory (1974) provides a comprehensive survey and critique of competing Marxist explanations of crisis, defending the falling-rate-of-profit thesis against underconsumptionist and disproportionality theories.

Mattick's work gained renewed attention after the 2008 financial crisis, as his analysis of the limits of Keynesian intervention appeared vindicated by events. His son, Paul Mattick Jr., has continued the theoretical project with Business as Usual (2011), applying his father's framework to contemporary capitalism.

name: Paul Sweezy
body: Paul Sweezy was an American Marxist economist and co-founder of Monthly Review, one of the most enduring independent socialist publications in the United States. His early Theory of Capitalist Development provided the first systematic English-language treatment of Marxist economics, while Monopoly Capital, co-authored with Paul Baran, argued that the concentration of corporate power had produced a tendency toward economic stagnation counteracted only by military spending, advertising, and financial speculation. His work established a distinctive school of Marxist political economy focused on monopoly, imperialism, and the structural tendencies of mature capitalism.

name: Perry Anderson
body:
Perry Anderson is a British Marxist historian and essayist who served as editor of New Left Review for several decades, shaping it into one of the most important journals of Marxist intellectual life. His work spans historical sociology, political theory, and the history of ideas, combining analytical rigor with a sweeping command of European history and social theory.

In Lineages of the Absolutist State (1974) and its companion volume Passages from Antiquity to Feudalism, Anderson offered a systematic Marxist account of state formation in Europe and the transition from feudalism to capitalism. Considerations on Western Marxism (1976) provided an influential mapping of the shift in Marxist theory from political economy and strategy toward philosophy and aesthetics after the defeats of the interwar period. Anderson's work is notable for its insistence on combining structural analysis with attention to geopolitical and conjunctural specificity, and for its sustained engagement with questions of state power, class formation, and historical transition.

name: Pierre Leroux
body:
Pierre Leroux was a French philosopher and political thinker who is often credited with coining the word "socialism" in 1834. Originally a follower of Saint-Simon, he broke with the Saint-Simonian movement and developed his own philosophical system blending social criticism with religious and humanistic themes. He served in the French National Assembly after the 1848 revolution and spent years in exile following Louis-Napoleon's coup.

In his major work On Humanity, Leroux argued that human progress depends on the recognition of solidarity and mutual dependence among individuals. He rejected both liberal individualism and authoritarian collectivism, seeking a synthesis grounded in a quasi-religious conception of humanity's spiritual unity. While Marx criticized Leroux's idealism, Leroux's emphasis on the social nature of human existence and his critique of competitive individualism contributed to the broader intellectual ferment from which scientific socialism emerged.

name: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon
body:
Pierre-Joseph Proudhon was a French socialist theorist and the first person to call himself an anarchist. His declaration that 'property is theft' in What Is Property? (1840) made him famous, while his mutualist economic programme proposed replacing capitalism with a system of free credit and cooperative exchange without state intervention.

Marx initially admired Proudhon's critique of property but broke with him decisively in The Poverty of Philosophy (1847), a polemical response to Proudhon's The Philosophy of Poverty. Marx criticised Proudhon for failing to understand the labour theory of value, for proposing to reform commodity exchange rather than abolish it, and for substituting utopian schemes for scientific analysis of capitalist production. The Marx-Proudhon conflict established the fundamental divide between Marxist and anarchist traditions that would split the First International. Proudhon's mutualism, his opposition to strikes, and his reactionary views on women and race have continued to generate debate within the broader socialist movement.

name: Ralph Miliband
body:
Ralph Miliband was a Belgian-born British political sociologist and Marxist theorist whose work on the capitalist state made him one of the most important political thinkers of the postwar British left. A professor at the London School of Economics and later at Leeds University, he founded the Socialist Register annual in 1964 and edited it for three decades.

In The State in Capitalist Society (1969), Miliband argued against pluralist political science by demonstrating that the state in advanced capitalist countries systematically serves the interests of the capitalist class, not because of conspiracy but through the social origins of state personnel, the structural dependence of the state on capital, and the ideological hegemony of the dominant class. His debate with Nicos Poulantzas over whether the state should be analyzed instrumentally or structurally became one of the defining theoretical exchanges in Marxist political theory. Miliband's insistence on combining empirical investigation with theoretical analysis of state power remains influential in Marxist political sociology.

name: Raya Dunayevskaya
body:
Raya Dunayevskaya was a Ukrainian-American Marxist philosopher who founded the tradition of Marxist-Humanism in the United States. She served briefly as Leon Trotsky's secretary before breaking with Trotskyism to develop her theory that the Soviet Union represented a form of state capitalism rather than a deformed workers' state. She co-led the Johnson-Forest Tendency with C.L.R. James before establishing her own organization, News and Letters Committees.

In Marxism and Freedom (1958) and Philosophy and Revolution (1973), Dunayevskaya argued that Marx's philosophical humanism, rooted in Hegel's dialectic, was inseparable from his economic analysis. She insisted that the movement from practice, particularly the self-activity of workers, women, and colonized peoples, constitutes a form of theory. Her recovery of Marx's 1844 Manuscripts and her emphasis on the Hegelian foundations of Marx's thought contributed to a broader reassessment of the relationship between Marxist philosophy and revolutionary practice.

name: Raymond Williams
body:
Raymond Williams was a Welsh cultural theorist, novelist, and critic who was one of the founding figures of cultural studies and a leading representative of the British New Left. Born into a working-class family in a Welsh border village, he studied at Cambridge and became a prolific writer whose work transformed the study of culture within the Marxist tradition.

In Culture and Society (1958) and The Long Revolution (1961), Williams traced the development of the idea of culture in English thought, arguing that culture is not a separate aesthetic realm but an integral dimension of social life. His concept of "cultural materialism" sought to analyze cultural production as a material social process, overcoming the base-superstructure model that reduced culture to a mere reflection of economic relations. In Marxism and Literature (1977), he developed concepts such as "structures of feeling" and the distinction between dominant, residual, and emergent cultural formations that provided tools for analyzing the lived experience of social change.

name: Robert Brenner
body:
Robert Brenner is an American Marxist historian and economist at UCLA who is a central figure in the tradition of Political Marxism. His interventions in debates over the transition from feudalism to capitalism and the dynamics of contemporary global capitalism have been among the most influential in late twentieth-century Marxist thought.

In what became known as the "Brenner Debate," he argued that the transition to capitalism cannot be explained by demographic pressure, expanding trade, or technological change, but only by the transformation of class-property relations in the English countryside that made producers dependent on the market. In The Economics of Global Turbulence (2006), Brenner offered an account of the long downturn in the world economy since the 1970s rooted in overcapacity and intensified international competition rather than in the falling rate of profit or financialization alone. His work insists on the primacy of social-property relations and class conflict as the drivers of economic development and crisis.

name: Robert Owen
body:
Robert Owen was a Welsh industrialist and social reformer whose experiments in cooperative living made him one of the founding figures of utopian socialism. As manager of the New Lanark cotton mills in Scotland, he demonstrated that improving workers' conditions could be compatible with profitability, introducing shorter hours, better housing, and schooling for workers' children. He later attempted to establish a model community at New Harmony, Indiana, which ultimately failed.

Owen argued that human character is formed by social environment rather than innate nature, and that rational reorganization of society along cooperative lines would eliminate poverty, crime, and ignorance. Engels identified Owen as one of the three great utopian socialists alongside Fourier and Saint-Simon. While Marx criticized Owen's reliance on moral persuasion rather than class struggle, Owen's practical experiments in cooperative production and his labor theory of value influenced the early British labor movement and the cooperative tradition.

name: Roman Rosdolsky
body:
Roman Rosdolsky was a Ukrainian Marxist scholar and political activist whose study of Marx's Grundrisse became a foundational text for the renaissance of Marxist economic theory. Active in the revolutionary movement in interwar Galicia, he survived the Auschwitz concentration camp and emigrated to the United States, where he spent his remaining years in scholarly work.

His major work, The Making of Marx's Capital (1968), was among the first comprehensive studies of the Grundrisse, which had only recently been published in full. Rosdolsky traced the development of Marx's plan for Capital from the original six-book outline through its various transformations, illuminating the methodological foundations of Marx's critique of political economy. His careful reconstruction of Marx's categories of value, money, and capital demonstrated the centrality of the Hegelian dialectic to Marx's mature economic thought and helped establish the Grundrisse as an essential text for understanding Marx's intellectual project.

name: Rosa Luxemburg
body: Rosa Luxemburg advanced an influential theory of capitalist accumulation that argued capitalism structurally depends on the incorporation of non-capitalist markets and territories for the realization of surplus value. Her political writings mounted a sustained critique of reformism within the social-democratic movement and theorized the mass strike as a form of revolutionary working-class self-activity. She was murdered by Freikorps paramilitaries in Berlin in January 1919.

name: Rudolf Hilferding
body:
Rudolf Hilferding was an Austrian Marxist economist and politician whose Finance Capital (1910) provided one of the most influential analyses of the transformation of competitive capitalism into monopoly capitalism. Hilferding argued that the concentration of industrial production and the centralization of banking had produced a new form of capital — finance capital — in which banks assumed a dominant role in directing industrial investment, organizing cartels and trusts, and driving the imperialist expansion of capital into colonial territories. His analysis of the joint-stock company, the credit system, and the stock exchange as mechanisms through which finance capital coordinates and controls the productive economy anticipated many features of twentieth-century capitalism.

Hilferding's earlier work, Bohm-Bawerk's Criticism of Marx (1904), mounted a systematic defense of Marx's value theory against the marginalist critique, and remains one of the most rigorous engagements between Marxist and neoclassical economics. His concept of "organized capitalism" — the thesis that monopoly and state intervention had created the possibility of conscious economic planning within capitalism — influenced both social-democratic reformism and later debates about state capitalism.

Hilferding served twice as finance minister of the Weimar Republic. He fled Germany after the Nazi seizure of power, was captured by the Vichy police in 1941, and died in Gestapo custody in Paris. His theoretical legacy endures through Lenin's Imperialism, which drew heavily on Finance Capital, and through contemporary analyses of financialization.

name: Samir Amin
body:
Samir Amin was an Egyptian-French economist and one of the most prominent theorists of dependency and unequal exchange in the Marxist tradition. Born in Cairo and educated in Paris, Amin spent most of his career in Dakar, Senegal, where he directed the Third World Forum and the African Institute for Economic Development and Planning, combining theoretical work with practical engagement in African economic policy and South-South cooperation.

Amin's Accumulation on a World Scale (1970) argues that the capitalist world system is structured by a fundamental asymmetry between center and periphery, in which the peripheral economies are not merely "behind" the advanced countries on a common developmental path but are structurally dependent — their underdevelopment is the necessary counterpart of metropolitan accumulation. Drawing on Marx's theory of value and extending it to the international division of labor, Amin developed a theory of unequal exchange in which the transfer of value from periphery to center is built into the structure of world trade, perpetuating global inequality regardless of the development strategies adopted by individual nations.

His concept of "delinking" — the strategic disengagement of peripheral economies from the world market to pursue autonomous, nationally determined development — challenged both free-trade orthodoxy and the Soviet model of integration into a socialist world market. Eurocentrism (1988) extended his critique to the ideological domain, arguing that Western social science universalizes the particular historical experience of Europe and naturalizes capitalist modernity as the telos of all human development. Amin's work remains a central reference for debates about globalization, imperialism, and the prospects for development in the Global South.

name: Silvia Federici
body: Silvia Federici is an Italian-American scholar and activist whose work has fundamentally reshaped Marxist understandings of gender, reproduction, and primitive accumulation. In Caliban and the Witch, she argued that the transition to capitalism required the violent subjugation of women and the enclosure of reproductive labor, reinterpreting the European witch hunts as a constitutive moment of capitalist accumulation. Her earlier agitation in the Wages for Housework campaign brought international attention to the unwaged domestic labor that sustains capitalist production yet remains invisible within orthodox political economy.

name: Slavoj Zizek
body:
Slavoj Zizek is a Slovenian philosopher and cultural critic who has become one of the most prominent and controversial public intellectuals on the contemporary left. His theoretical project synthesizes Lacanian psychoanalysis, Hegelian dialectics, and Marxist ideology critique into a distinctive philosophical framework that has been applied to politics, cinema, theology, and popular culture with restless, encyclopedic energy.

Zizek's breakthrough work, The Sublime Object of Ideology (1989), argues that ideology functions not through false consciousness — people mistakenly believing what is not true — but through a cynical distance that is itself ideological. Drawing on Lacan's concept of jouissance (enjoyment) and Marx's analysis of commodity fetishism, Zizek contends that subjects can know perfectly well that their social reality is structured by illusion and yet continue to act as if they did not know, because ideology is embedded in practice rather than belief. The formula is not "they do not know what they do" but "they know very well what they are doing, and they do it anyway." This insight has been enormously productive for analyzing how power operates in liberal democracies and consumer societies where explicit ideological commitment has been replaced by performative compliance and ironic detachment.

His later works, particularly Less Than Nothing (2012), undertake a comprehensive rereading of Hegel through Lacan, arguing that dialectical materialism must be reconstructed on the basis of psychoanalytic theory. Critics accuse Zizek of intellectual inconsistency, political ambiguity, and a tendency to substitute theoretical provocation for sustained political engagement, while admirers regard him as the most inventive dialectician of his generation.

name: Stuart Hall
body:
Stuart Hall was a Jamaican-born British cultural theorist and sociologist who was one of the most influential intellectuals of the postwar British left. A founding editor of New Left Review and longtime director of the Centre for Contemporary Cultural Studies at the University of Birmingham, he played a pivotal role in establishing cultural studies as an academic discipline and in bringing questions of race, identity, and popular culture into Marxist analysis.

Hall drew on Gramsci's concept of hegemony to analyze how consent is manufactured through cultural institutions, media, and everyday practices. In Policing the Crisis (1978), he and his co-authors analyzed how the moral panic around mugging served to legitimate authoritarian state measures. His analysis of Thatcherism as an "authoritarian populism" that restructured common sense demonstrated how the right could win consent for policies that served ruling-class interests. Hall's insistence that race is not reducible to class, but is articulated with it in specific historical conjunctures, expanded the analytical reach of Marxist cultural theory.

name: Terry Eagleton
body: Terry Eagleton is a British literary theorist and cultural critic who has done more than perhaps any other figure to make Marxist ideas accessible to a broad English-speaking readership. His Literary Theory: An Introduction became one of the most widely read introductions to the field, situating various theoretical schools within their political and ideological contexts. Throughout his prolific career, he has defended the continuing relevance of Marxist critique against postmodernist and liberal challenges, combining theoretical rigor with polemical wit.

name: Theodor W. Adorno
body: Theodor W. Adorno was a German philosopher, sociologist, and musicologist who became one of the most prominent members of the Frankfurt School. With Max Horkheimer, he authored Dialectic of Enlightenment, which traced how the Enlightenment's drive to dominate nature had paradoxically produced new forms of domination and barbarism. His later Negative Dialectics sought to sustain critical thought without resolving contradictions into a totalizing synthesis, insisting that philosophy must attend to the non-identical and the particular.

name: Thomas Malthus
key_works: An Essay on the Principle of Population
body:
Thomas Malthus was a British clergyman and political economist whose Essay on the Principle of Population (1798) argued that population growth inevitably outstrips the growth of food supply, leading to poverty, famine, and social misery. He used this thesis to oppose poor relief and radical reform, contending that the suffering of the lower classes was a natural law rather than a consequence of social organization.

Marx regarded Malthus as one of the most ideologically motivated of the classical economists, a shameless apologist for the landed aristocracy who deployed pseudo-scientific arguments to justify inequality. Marx countered Malthus's population theory by arguing that what appears as overpopulation under capitalism is actually a relative surplus population produced by the dynamics of capital accumulation itself. The reserve army of labor is not a natural demographic phenomenon but a structural feature of the capitalist mode of production. Malthus's influence persists as a foil against which Marxist analysis of poverty and crisis continues to define itself.

name: Vladimir Lenin
key_works: Imperialism the Highest Stage of Capitalism
body: Vladimir Lenin theorized imperialism as a structural stage of capitalist development characterized by the dominance of finance capital, monopoly, and the territorial division of the world among great powers. His The State and Revolution reinterpreted Marx and Engels on the class character of the state and the necessity of its destruction in the revolutionary process. As the principal leader of the October Revolution of 1917, his political and theoretical legacy shaped the trajectory of twentieth-century Marxism worldwide.

name: Walter Benjamin
key_works: The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction
Theses on the Philosophy of History
body: Walter Benjamin was a German literary critic and philosopher whose eclectic body of work combined Marxist materialism with Jewish messianic theology. His essay on mechanical reproduction analyzed how technologies like photography and film transformed the nature of art by destroying its traditional aura of uniqueness and authenticity. His final work, the Theses on the Philosophy of History, offered a radical critique of progressive historicism, insisting that revolution emerges not from historical inevitability but from a redemptive interruption of catastrophic continuity.

name: Wilhelm Weitling
body:
Wilhelm Weitling was a German tailor and early communist theorist whose Guarantees of Harmony and Freedom (1842) was one of the first works of German socialism. Self-educated and from the artisan class, Weitling developed a utopian communist programme combining Christian egalitarianism with proposals for the abolition of private property and money.

Marx initially praised Weitling's work as evidence of the German proletariat's theoretical capacity but broke with him at the famous 1846 Brussels meeting, where Marx insisted that communist politics must be grounded in scientific analysis rather than moral appeals and conspiratorial schemes. Weitling represented the tradition of artisan communism — rooted in craft solidarity and millenarian egalitarianism — that Marx sought to supersede with historical materialism. After emigrating to the United States, Weitling's influence waned, but his career illustrates the pre-Marxist communist tradition from which scientific socialism emerged and against which it defined itself.

name: William Petty
body:
William Petty was an English economist and polymath whom Marx called the founder of political economy in England. A physician, surveyor, and member of Parliament, Petty pioneered the use of quantitative methods in economic analysis, which he termed "political arithmetic." His Treatise of Taxes and Contributions (1662) examined the sources of state revenue and the nature of economic value.

Petty advanced one of the earliest formulations of the labor theory of value, arguing that labor and land together constitute the basis of wealth. He sought to express the value of all commodities in terms of these two fundamental factors. Marx regarded Petty's work as a crucial early step toward understanding value as rooted in labor, despite its unsystematic character. Petty's attempts to measure national wealth, analyze the division of labor, and understand the relationship between money and commodities anticipated problems that would be central to classical political economy and to Marx's own critique of it.
